mnemonics.title: 2NF Rule
mnemonics.content: 2NF = 1NF + No Partial Dependency: Remember 'Part of Key → Non-Key' = BAD

mnemonics.title: 3NF Rule
mnemonics.content: 3NF = 2NF + No Transitive Dependency: Remember 'Non-Key → Non-Key' = BAD

mnemonics.title: Normalization Order
mnemonics.content: Think of NF as stairs: 1NF→2NF→3NF→BCNF - each step eliminates specific 'bad' dependencies

acronyms.usage: Eliminate partial dependencies where non-prime attributes depend on part of a candidate key
Eliminate transitive dependencies where non-prime attributes depend on other non-prime attributes
Stricter 3NF where every determinant must be a superkey
Describes relationship between attributes where one attribute determines another
A chosen candidate key used to uniquely identify tuples in a relation

visualTips.visualization: Draw the dependency diagram - if any arrow points from part of key to non-key, that's partial dependency. Split into relations: (key part)→(non-key) and (whole key + other attributes)
Identify transitive chains: Key→A→B where A and B are non-prime. Break by creating relation (Key, A) and relation (A, B)
Circle all attributes in candidate keys - these are prime. Everything else outside the circles is non-prime.

analogies.analogy: Like a building where the roof (non-key) depends on only one pillar (part of composite key) rather than the entire foundation - unstable!
Like borrowing money through a friend - you don't know the lender directly, creating an indirect unstable chain.

Organizing a library: 1NF puts all books in individual shelves (atomic), 2NF removes books that belong to other sections on wrong shelves, 3NF ensures no book category description appears in multiple places.
